title: UPSC Civil Services 2026
meta_description: UPSC CSE 2026 — complete guide to IAS/IPS/IFS exam. Prelims May 24, Mains Aug 21, exam pattern, syllabus overview, salary ₹56K-2.5L/month, interview.

hero.one_liner: India's most prestigious examination — gateway to IAS, IPS, IFS, and 20+ other All India and Central Services

what_is_it: The UPSC Civil Services Examination (CSE) is conducted annually by the Union Public Service Commission to recruit officers for the Indian Administrative Service (IAS), Indian Police Service (IPS), Indian Foreign Service (IFS), and 20+ other Group A and Group B services. It is widely regarded as the toughest and most competitive exam in India, with 10-13 lakh applicants competing for about 1,000 vacancies.

The examination has three stages: Preliminary Exam (objective, screening), Main Exam (written, descriptive — 9 papers over 5 days), and Interview/Personality Test. The entire process takes about 12-14 months from Prelims to final results. The 2026 UPSC Prelims is scheduled for 24 May 2026, and Mains for 21 August 2026.

Successful candidates join elite services that shape India's governance. IAS officers become District Collectors/Magistrates, Secretaries to the Government, and eventually Chief Secretaries. IPS officers head police forces from SP to DGP level. IFS officers serve as Ambassadors and High Commissioners across the world. The starting salary at the entry level (Level 10) is approximately ₹56,100/month, but with allowances (DA, HRA, TA), the in-hand salary ranges from ₹80,000 to ₹1,00,000+/month from day one, with free housing, vehicle, and other perquisites.

Unlike most other competitive exams, UPSC CSE tests not just knowledge but analytical thinking, decision-making ability, ethics, and leadership potential. The syllabus encompasses everything from ancient history to current affairs, from economics to science, from governance to international relations — it's essentially a test of being a well-rounded, informed citizen of India.

key_details.label: Education
key_details.value: Bachelor's degree from any recognized university (any stream — Arts, Science, Commerce, Engineering, Medicine, etc.)

key_details.label: Age Limit (Gen)
key_details.value: 21–32 years. OBC: up to 35 years. SC/ST: up to 37 years. PwBD: up to 42 years.

key_details.label: Number of Attempts
key_details.value: Gen: 6 attempts, OBC: 9 attempts, SC/ST: unlimited (within age limit)

key_details.label: Application Fee
key_details.value: ₹100 (Exempted for Women, SC, ST, and PwBD candidates)

key_details.label: Exam Mode
key_details.value: Prelims: Offline (OMR), Mains: Offline (pen and paper), Interview: In-person at UPSC Bhawan, Delhi

key_details.label: Services recruited
key_details.value: IAS, IPS, IFS, IRS, IRTS, IRAS, IAAS, ICAS, IDES, IIS, IPoS, IPrS, IOFS, and more — total 24 services

exam_pattern.description: Prelims is a screening test — only to shortlist candidates for Mains. Prelims marks are NOT counted in final ranking. Paper 1 (General Studies) is for merit ranking, Paper 2 (CSAT) is qualifying only (33% needed). About 12-14× the number of vacancies are shortlisted for Mains.

subjects.name: Paper 2: CSAT — Aptitude (2 hours, qualifying — 33% needed)

exam_pattern.negative_marking: 1/3rd of marks for each wrong answer (0.66 marks deducted per wrong answer in Paper 1)

exam_pattern.description: Mains is the core exam that determines your merit ranking. It consists of 9 papers — 7 are counted for ranking (1,750 marks) and 2 are qualifying (language papers). Papers include 4 General Studies papers, 1 Essay, 1 Optional Subject (2 papers), and 2 language papers. All papers are descriptive (essay-type answers), written in 3 hours each.

subjects.name: Paper 2: GS-I History, Geography, Society (250 marks)
Paper 3: GS-II Governance, Polity, IR (250 marks)
Paper 4: GS-III Economy, Science, Environment (250 marks)
Paper 5: GS-IV Ethics, Integrity, Aptitude (250 marks)

major_posts.department: All India Service — posted across states
All India Service — SP to DGP
Ministry of External Affairs — Embassies worldwide

extra_sections.heading: Prelims Syllabus — Key Topics
content: Paper 1 — General Studies (200 marks, 100 questions):
Current events of national and international importance. History of India and Indian National Movement.

Indian and World Geography — physical, social, economic geography of India and the World. Indian Polity and Governance — Constitution, political system, Panchayati Raj, public policy, rights issues. Economic and Social Development — sustainable development, poverty, inclusion, demographics, social sector initiatives.
General issues on Environmental Ecology, Bio-diversity, and Climate Change. General Science.
Paper 2 — CSAT (200 marks, 80 questions, qualifying — need 33%):
Comprehension. Interpersonal skills including communication skills.
Logical reasoning and analytical ability. Decision making and problem solving.
General mental ability. Basic numeracy (numbers and their relations, orders of magnitude, etc.) — Class 10 level.
Data interpretation (charts, graphs, tables, data sufficiency etc.) — Class 10 level.
Key Prelims Strategy: Paper 1 decides if you qualify for Mains. Focus 80% of Prelims preparation on Paper 1.
For Paper 2 (CSAT), just practice enough to score 33% comfortably — don't waste excessive time here. Current affairs of the last 12 months is the single biggest scoring area in Paper 1.

extra_sections.heading: Optional Subject Selection Guide
rows: Sociology, Geography, Political Science, Public Admin
Civil Eng, Mechanical Eng, Electrical Eng

extra_sections.heading: How to Choose Your Optional Subject
content: The Optional Subject (Mains Paper 6 & 7, total 500 marks) is crucial — it can make or break your rank. Here's how to choose:
Popular optional subjects with high scoring potential: Sociology, Geography, Public Administration, Political Science, Anthropology, History, Philosophy, and Psychology.
These are popular because they overlap with GS papers and have manageable syllabus sizes.

Engineering/Science backgrounds: Many engineers choose Mathematics, Medical Science, or their engineering subject as optional. These can be high-scoring but also high-risk — one bad paper can cost 50-60 marks.
Literature optionals: Available in 25+ Indian languages and English. Good for candidates strong in their mother tongue.
Less competition but scoring depends heavily on the examiner's preferences.

Key decision factors: (1) Interest in the subject — you'll spend 4-6 months studying it deeply, (2) Overlap with GS papers — Sociology, Geography, and Polity overlap well, (3) Availability of study material and coaching, (4) Scoring trends — check previous year toppers' optionals and average scores, (5) Syllabus size — smaller syllabuses like Anthropology and Sociology are easier to complete.
Most recommended for first-time aspirants: Sociology or Geography — manageable syllabus, good overlap with GS, abundant study material, and consistent scoring history.

extra_sections.heading: Interview — The Game Changer
content: The Interview carries 275 marks and is conducted at UPSC Bhawan, New Delhi. The board consists of a Chairman and 4-5 members.
Interview lasts 25-35 minutes.

What they test: Not academic knowledge (that's already tested in Mains). The interview assesses: mental alertness, critical powers of assimilation, clear and logical exposition, balance of judgment, variety and depth of interest, social cohesion, leadership, and intellectual and moral integrity.

Common topics: Your educational background and work experience, your home state (geography, culture, issues), current affairs (national and international), your DAF (Detailed Application Form) — every detail you filled in your form can be a question, ethical dilemmas and hypothetical administrative scenarios.
How to prepare: Read about your state comprehensively. Be ready to discuss every word in your DAF — hobbies, graduation subject, work experience.
Practice mock interviews with seniors or coaching institutes. Be honest — if you don't know something, say so clearly.
The board values honesty over bluffing.
Scoring: Average interview score is 55-60% (150-165 out of 275). A very good interview can get 190-210.
A poor one can drop to 100-120. The interview can swing your final rank by 50-100 positions — it's a game changer.

preparation_tips: Start with NCERT textbooks Class 6-12 for History, Geography, Science, Economics, and Polity. This is non-negotiable — NCERTs form the foundation for both Prelims and Mains. Spend the first 2-3 months just reading NCERTs.
Read 'Indian Polity' by M. Laxmikanth cover-to-cover. This single book covers 60-70% of the Polity questions in Prelims and is essential for GS Paper 2 in Mains.

Subscribe to one newspaper (The Hindu or Indian Express) and read it daily — focus on editorials, economy news, and international relations. Make short notes of important events. Current affairs is the highest-scoring area in Prelims.
Choose your optional subject early (within first 2 months) and start studying it alongside GS. Don't keep changing optionals — consistency matters more than the 'perfect' choice.

Answer writing practice is THE most important Mains preparation activity. From month 4 onwards, write at least 2-3 answers daily in a time-bound manner. Get them evaluated by a mentor, coaching institute, or peer group. Many toppers attribute their success to consistent answer writing practice.

Join a test series for both Prelims and Mains. For Prelims, take at minimum 30-40 full-length tests. Analyze every test thoroughly — understanding why you got wrong answers is more valuable than the test itself.

Don't underestimate the Ethics paper (GS-IV, 250 marks). It's the easiest GS paper to score well in if you practice case studies. Read 'Ethics, Integrity, and Aptitude' by Lexicon and practice 2-3 case studies daily from month 6 onwards.

Stay consistent rather than studying in bursts. 6-8 hours of focused daily study for 12-18 months is the typical preparation timeline for a working professional or fresh graduate. Full-time aspirants may prepare in 10-12 months.

faqs.question: What is the minimum qualification for UPSC CSE?
faqs.answer: A Bachelor's degree from any recognized university in ANY subject. There's no minimum percentage requirement. Final year students can also appear for Prelims — but must have the degree by the time of Mains. Even distance/correspondence degrees are accepted as long as the university is UGC-recognized.

faqs.question: How many attempts do I get?
faqs.answer: General: 6 attempts (till age 32). OBC: 9 attempts (till age 35). SC/ST: Unlimited attempts (till age 37). PwBD General: 9 attempts (till age 42). PwBD OBC: 9 attempts (till age 42). PwBD SC/ST: Unlimited (till age 42). Each appearance at Prelims counts as one attempt — even if you don't qualify.

faqs.question: Can I crack UPSC without coaching?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many toppers including AIR 1 holders have prepared without formal coaching. With NCERT books, standard references, free YouTube lectures (Mrunal, ForumIAS, Unacademy free content), and a good test series, self-preparation is viable. However, coaching can provide structure, peer group, and answer evaluation — especially valuable for working professionals with limited time.

faqs.question: What is the UPSC CSE salary?
faqs.answer: Entry level (Junior Scale, Level 10): Basic pay ₹56,100/month + DA (currently ~50%) + HRA (if no government accommodation) + TA. Total in-hand: ₹80,000-1,00,000/month. After 4-5 years (Senior Scale): ₹1,20,000-1,50,000/month. At Secretary/Additional Secretary level: ₹2,00,000-2,50,000/month. Plus: free government accommodation, vehicle, domestic help, medical, LTC, children's education allowance.

faqs.question: IAS vs IPS vs IFS — how is service allocation done?
faqs.answer: Based on your final rank and preference list. Top-ranked candidates get their first choice. Typically, ranks 1-100 get IAS/IFS (depending on preference). Ranks 100-300 get IPS. Ranks 300-800 get other Group A services like IRS, IRTS, etc. Exact cutoffs change every year based on vacancies and the number of candidates. You submit your preference order during registration — you can prefer IPS over IAS if that's your goal.

faqs.question: Is UPSC really the toughest exam?
faqs.answer: In terms of the breadth of syllabus (virtually everything), duration of process (12-14 months), and competition (10 lakh+ applicants for 1,000 seats), UPSC CSE is uniquely challenging. However, 'toughest' is subjective — GATE, CAT, and JEE Advanced are tougher in their specific domains. What makes UPSC unique is that it tests general awareness, analytical thinking, writing ability, and personality — not just technical knowledge.

faqs.question: How long does preparation take?
faqs.answer: Typical timeline: 12-18 months of dedicated preparation for a serious first attempt. Some clear it in their first attempt with 10-12 months of focused study. Others take 2-3 attempts spanning 3-5 years. The key is quality of preparation, not just duration. 6-8 hours of focused study daily is the standard benchmark for serious aspirants.

image_suggestions: IAS officer at desk with flag

syllabus.subject: General Studies Paper I
syllabus.topics: Indian heritage & culture, history (modern India, world history), geography (India & world — physical, social, economic), Indian society — features, diversity, social empowerment, communalism, regionalism, secularism

syllabus.subject: CSAT (Paper II — Qualifying)
syllabus.topics: Comprehension, interpersonal skills, logical reasoning & analytical ability, decision making, general mental ability, basic numeracy, data interpretation (class X level)

syllabus.subject: Mains: Essay
syllabus.topics: Two essays from different topics — philosophical, socio-economic, political, current affairs. Each essay 1000-1200 words. Tests depth of thought, clarity, and structured argumentation.

syllabus.subject: Mains: GS Paper I-IV
syllabus.topics: GS1: History, geography, society. GS2: Governance, constitution, polity, international relations. GS3: Economy, science & tech, environment, disaster management. GS4: Ethics, integrity, aptitude — case studies.

study_plan.tasks: Read ALL NCERTs: History (6th-12th), Geography (6th-12th), Science (6th-10th), Polity (11th-12th), Economy (11th-12th). Make short notes from each chapter. This is the foundation — don't skip.

Laxmikanth Polity (cover to cover — most important book). Bipan Chandra Modern History. Ramesh Singh Economy (focus on budget, monetary policy, banking). G.C. Leong Geography. Start reading newspaper (The Hindu/Indian Express) daily — 1 hour.

Start writing GS Mains answers daily (2-3 answers, 250 words each). Join a test series (Vision IAS, Forum IAS). Current affairs monthly magazines (Yojana, Kurukshetra, Down to Earth). Ethics case studies practice.
Solve last 10 years Prelims papers. Take weekly mock tests. Focus on elimination technique — in UPSC Prelims, eliminating 2 wrong options is often enough. Revise NCERTs and static portions.

Daily revision of notes. 2 mock tests per week. Focus on current affairs of last 6 months. No new books — only revision. Stay calm, eat well, sleep 7 hours. The exam tests temperament as much as knowledge.

quiz.question: Which Schedule of the Constitution contains the list of states and union territories?
